items.correct: Holding minimal stock and receiving inputs only when needed for production — reducing storage costs but increasing vulnerability to supply disruption.
items.misconception: Just-in-time means delivering products to customers exactly on time.
items.random_wrong: Just-in-time means finishing work right before the deadline.
items.why: JIT is about INPUT stock to production, not output delivery to customers. Students misread the term as a delivery promise; it is an internal stock control philosophy.

items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: Why are hygiene factors and motivators not on the same scale in Herzberg's theory?
items.correct: Hygiene factors only prevent dissatisfaction (their absence demotivates, presence is neutral); motivators create satisfaction (their presence motivates, absence is neutral). They affect different outcomes, not different points on one scale.
items.misconception: Hygiene factors and motivators are just two types of motivation that combine.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness only.
items.why: Herzberg's key insight is dual-factor: satisfaction and dissatisfaction are not opposites but separate dimensions. Students collapse them to a single motivation scale, missing the structural claim.

items.q: Current assets $60k, current liabilities $30k. Current ratio is:
items.why: Correct answer: 2.0. “0.5” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Profitability vs capital. “Liquidity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Operating profit $40k, capital employed $200k. ROCE (%) is:
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
